title: Quantification of Phytoplankton Cells with Preys Using Flow Cytometry
author: Valeria Jimenez
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.be2vjge6

steps.instruction: Prepare your prey (YG-beads or FLBs) stock solution. Vortex vigorously and sonicate the solution for ~60 seconds.

Take the phytoplankton flasks under different culture conditions (e.g. phytoplankton incubated in the dark and under nutrient limitation) on which to test uptake of prey (use at least 2 to 3 replicates per phytoplankton strain and culture condition)

Add the preys to each flask (one flask at a time). After microsphere addition, gently mix the flask to evenly distribute microspheres and then take a sub-sample from the flask for immediate fixation (see fixation protocol section). This sub-sample is the T0 for background correction (attachment of preys to cells not associated with mixotrophy) Preys (Microspheres or FLBs) added to attain a preys to cells ratio of 1 to 2. The addition of preys and subsequent fixation can be done in the same culture flask (sacrifice culture) or by taking an aliquot (e.g. 5 ml) into a culture tube or scintillation vials. The following protocol is written using culture flasks.

After the addition of preys and the sampling and fixation of the T0 sample, each flask with preys can be sampled and fixed over time (After 20 (T20) and 40 (T40) minutes incubation with preys) as performed with T0 sample.
After all flasks and time points are fixed, samples are run in the flow cytometer the same day.
The following fixation protocol must be done under a hood. The volume of reagents must be added to 5 ml of culture.
Dissolve 10 g of Potassium iodide in 100 ml distilled water
Add 3 ml of sodium thiosulfate in 97 ml of distilled water
After all flasks and time points are fixed, samples are kept on ice are in the dark to be run in the flow cytometer the same day.
Add 300 µl of 37% Formaldehyde to the sample.

Add 50 µl 3% Thiosulfate (see fixatives preparation section) to the sample. Make sure the sample is thoroughly cleared of Lugol’s stain, if not add more sodium thiosulfate until it is clear (e.g. another 50 µl)
Slowly add 5 g of iodine crystals. Shake to dissolve.
Add 10 ml glacial acetic acid. Store in the dark.

To determine the percent of cells with preys the sample is run with the threshold on red fluorescence. Cells without preys only show red autofluorescence from chlorophyll and the cells with the same chlorophyll fluorescence as well as green fluorescence (same signal as the prey added, YG-beads or FLBs) are considered to be cells containing prey.
In addition, to confirm the total concentration of prey added to each flask, the sample was also run with the threshold on green fluorescence

Figure of dot plots examples of flow cytometry data for Micromonas polaris and the positive control Ochromonas triangulata . Cells that display red autofluorescence from chlorophyll and green fluorescence are considered as cells containing prey (cells with YG-beads in green, cells with FLBs in orange and cells without prey in blue). In addition, to confirm the total concentration of prey added to each experimental flask, the same sample was also run with the threshold on green fluorescence (only YG-beads and FLBs in grey and black respectively)
Add 50 µl of acid Lugol’s iodine solution (see fixatives preparation section) to the sample.
